title: How to Explain Triggers to People Who Don't Have Them
researcherTopic: sensorimotor psychotherapy, the body in trauma treatment
metaDescription: Learn how to explain trauma triggers to those who don't have them by using metaphors and setting boundaries. A guide to translating the body's language.
imageDescription: Warm, luminous light filters through a window, illuminating two hands gently untangling a delicate golden thread. The scene evokes a sense of patient, careful communication and the mending of something intricate and precious.
htmlContent:
I've sat across from hundreds of people who describe the same impossible task: trying to build a bridge of words to a person standing on the other side of a chasm, a person who has never known the ground to shake. They are trying to explain a trigger, that sudden, bewildering internal weather system that shifts from calm to hurricane in a single breath, to someone who has only ever known clear skies. The conversation often ends in a quiet frustration, a sense of being fundamentally misunderstood that can feel almost as painful as the trigger itself, leaving one to wonder how to translate an experience that defies the very logic of language.

The Unseen Architecture of a Trigger

To begin, we must dismantle the common misconception that a trigger is simply a bad memory that can be managed with positive thinking. This is a profound misunderstanding of the mechanism, one that places the burden of a solution on the thinking mind, which is often the last to know what is happening. A trigger is not a thought or a recollection; it is a full-body, nervous-system-level event, a physiological reality that unfolds faster than conscious processing. It is the body’s threat-detection system, honed by the past, misfiring in the present—an echo of a time when a massive response was necessary for survival, playing out in a moment where it is no longer adaptive. It is procedural memory, a "how-to" guide for survival written in the language of sensation and impulse, not a declarative memory that can be recalled and narrated at will.

In her pioneering work in sensorimotor psychotherapy, Pat Ogden illuminates this territory, showing us how trauma is not stored as a neat narrative but is held in the very fabric of the body—in posture, movement, and deep, non-verbal sensations. The body, Ogden teaches, has its own memory system, independent of the story we tell ourselves. A trigger is the activation of this somatic memory. A scent, a tone of voice, a slant of light—these sensory fragments can bypass the cognitive brain and directly signal the nervous system to brace for impact, to fight, flee, or collapse. This is not a conscious choice. It is a reflex, as involuntary as pulling a hand away from a hot stove. Understanding this is the first step in moving the conversation from the memory’s content to the physiological response’s reality, a shift further explored when considering a somatic flashback.

Every resistance is information. The question is whether you're willing to read it.

This reframing is crucial. It moves the experience from the realm of psychological weakness to biological function. It is not a failure of character to be triggered; it is the success of a survival mechanism now operating on outdated information. When we see it this way, we can approach the experience not with shame, but with curiosity, a willingness to understand the intelligence beneath the chaos. What is this reaction trying to protect us from? What does it believe is happening? These questions open a door to a different kind of relationship with our internal world, based not on control, but on collaboration. It allows us to see the trigger not as an enemy to be vanquished, but as a messenger from a younger part of ourselves, trying to keep us safe with the only tools it has.

Translating the Language of the Nervous System

Explaining this internal reality requires translating the body's non-verbal language into the shared language of metaphor. One of the most effective is to compare the nervous system’s response to a faulty smoke alarm. A well-functioning alarm distinguishes between a house fire and shower steam. A traumatized nervous system is like an alarm with its sensitivity dialed all the way up. It screams with the same life-or-death intensity at burnt toast as it would at a house fire. The person knows, intellectually, that the threat is minor, but their body is screaming fire. The alarm and the physiological cascade of stress hormones are real, even if the threat is a phantom of the past. The smell of smoke is a sensory input, not a story, and the body reacts to the input, not the narrative we later construct about it.

Another useful metaphor is the time-traveling body. A trigger doesn’t just remind the mind of danger; it convinces the body the danger is happening now. The past collapses into the present. It is not remembering helplessness; it is inhabiting it. The heart pounds and breath shortens not because the brain is thinking a memory, but because the body is living it. This is a form of dissociation, a temporal disruption where the past is no longer "back there" but is "right here." This distinction is further illuminated by understanding the polyvagal ladder, which maps these physiological state shifts. The person isn't overreacting to the present; their body is accurately reacting to a past that has become the present.

We can also speak of it as an invisible injury, like an old fracture that aches when the weather changes. The bone has healed, but the tissue retains a sensitivity to atmospheric conditions. The person doesn’t control the ache; it’s a physiological response. So it is with a trigger. The original wound may be decades in the past, but the nervous system retains a deep sensitivity to certain cues. The ache that arises is not a choice but an embedded response pattern, a signal from a body that remembers the original impact with a fidelity the conscious mind can rarely match. It is a conversation between the present environment and the past experience, spoken in the body's native tongue.

The Cartography of Collapse and Activation

Part of the difficulty is that triggers don't have a single expression. The popular image is explosive anger or panic, but that’s only one territory on a wider map. The nervous system has multiple survival strategies, and a trigger can manifest as sympathetic activation—fight-or-flight—or as a profound dorsal vagal shutdown. It is not always loud. Sometimes, it is utterly silent. We can even experience a "functional freeze," where we continue to go through the motions of daily life—smiling, nodding, responding—while internally, we are completely checked out, a ghost in the machine of our own body.

A client once described this as having two settings: ‘scream’ or ‘disappear.’ The ‘scream’ is the sympathetic charge: racing heart, adrenaline, the impulse to push away or run—an experience of being overwhelmed by energy. But ‘disappear’ is also a trigger response. This is the collapse, the freeze, the dissociation. It can look like sudden fatigue, numbness, a flat affect, or an abrupt withdrawal. From the outside, it might be misinterpreted as disinterest or anger, when it is a sophisticated, involuntary protective mechanism. The system has detected a threat so overwhelming that fighting or fleeing are deemed useless, and the only option is to become small, still, and invisible.

This is a critical piece of the puzzle. We must help others understand that the sudden quietness or the way our eyes glaze over is not a comment on them. It is the nervous system pulling the emergency brake, a state of profound internal retreat to conserve energy. Without this map, the other person will interpret our behavior through their own lens and almost certainly get it wrong, seeing a choice where there is only a biological imperative, a personal rejection where there is only a desperate bid for safety. They are interacting with the tip of the iceberg, unaware of the massive, submerged structure of past experience that dictates its movement.

When Explanation Becomes Its Own Defense

There is a subtle trap we fall into: over-explaining. We become so invested in building that bridge of words that the explanation itself becomes a compulsive, frantic endeavor. We circle the same points, offer more evidence for our reality, and search the other’s face for a flicker of recognition that will finally allow us to feel sane. When that doesn’t come, the desperation can escalate. The explanation is no longer a generous act of connection but a desperate bid for survival. It becomes a performance for an audience we are not even sure is listening.

What is happening is that explaining has become a defense strategy, a form of fawning—the instinct to appease a perceived threat to prevent conflict or abandonment. When we compulsively explain our triggers, we are often subconsciously trying to manage the other person’s potential disbelief or judgment. We are trying to make our reality palatable enough that they will not turn away. The explanation is no longer for them; it is for us. It is a tool to regulate our own nervous system by controlling the external environment, a strategy that is both exhausting and ultimately doomed to fail because our safety cannot be outsourced.

The most sophisticated defense mechanism is the one that looks like wisdom.

This is a profound truth to sit with. The desire to be understood feels so pure. Articulating our experience feels like a step toward healing. And it can be. But when it becomes a compulsive loop, when our well-being hinges on whether the other person “gets it,” it has crossed into a different territory. It has become a reenactment of an old pattern, a belief that our safety depends on our ability to perform and be the perfect narrator of our own pain. The real work is not to become a better explainer, but to notice the impulse to over-explain as a signal that we are feeling unsafe and seeking external validation. There is a quiet grief in accepting that some people, no matter how much they love us, will never truly understand. But there is also a profound liberation in realizing we don't need them to in order to heal.

The Invitation: Shifting from Explanation to Embodied Boundaries

What if the goal was never for them to perfectly understand? What if the pursuit of their validation is a distraction from the real work? This is the invitation to turn our attention from the impossible task of transplanting our experience into another’s mind and toward the embodied work of honoring it for ourselves. The aim is not to make another person feel what we feel, but for us to build the capacity to be with what we feel and communicate our needs from that solid ground. This is the shift from explanation to embodied boundaries. Honoring it for ourselves looks like noticing the first flicker of activation and, instead of pushing it away, simply acknowledging it: "Ah, there you are." It is the practice of turning toward the sensation with a gentle curiosity, without needing to fix or change it.

An embodied boundary is not a wall, but a line drawn from a compassionate understanding of our own nervous system. It’s the difference between saying, “Can’t you see how much this hurts me?” and, “When this happens, I experience an overwhelming physiological response. I need space.” The first is a plea for empathy. The second is a clear, non-negotiable statement of need. It doesn’t require the other person to understand the ‘why’; it only asks them to respect the ‘what.’ This is a radical act of self-possession, a declaration that our well-being is not up for debate. It is the moment we stop auditioning for the role of "valid person" and simply claim our seat.

We are not our thoughts, but we are responsible for our relationship to them.

This shift requires building a relationship with our own internal world, learning to track the subtle physiological shifts that signal an approaching trigger. It requires getting quiet enough to hear the body’s first whisper of ‘no’ before it becomes a scream. When we can do this, we can intervene on our own behalf, learning to say, “I need to step away,” long before we are in collapse or activation. This is not a rejection of the other person, but a profound act of love for ourselves and the relationship, a chance to practice setting boundaries without guilt. It is choosing to regulate ourselves rather than asking someone else to do it for us.

The Architecture of a Supportive Conversation

When grounded in this new intention, the conversation itself has a different architecture. It’s no longer a trial but a collaborative exploration to create a safer relational space. This conversation must be held with intention, not in the aftermath of a trigger, but from a place of relative calm. Choose the time and place with care, a quiet moment where both can be present. Begin by stating the intention clearly: “I want to share something about my experience, not so you have to fix anything, but so we can understand each other better.” This lowers the stakes and removes the pressure for a ‘right’ reaction. It frames the conversation as a gift, not a demand.

From there, offer a metaphor. “You know how a smoke alarm can be overly sensitive? Sometimes my nervous system is like that.” This gives them a concrete hook. Then, connect the metaphor to a specific need. “When I get very quiet, it’s not because I’m angry. It’s my system’s alarm going off. In those moments, the most helpful thing is to give me quiet space without asking if I’m okay. I will come back when my system settles.” This is the crucial step: providing a clear, actionable instruction. We are telling them exactly how to love us better. It is also vital to tell them what is not helpful: trying to reason us out of it, telling us we are safe, or asking "why" we are triggered. These actions, though well-intentioned, can feel invalidating in the moment.

Finally, we must release our attachment to the outcome. We can offer the explanation and the need with clarity and kindness, but we cannot control how it is received. Their ability to meet us depends on their own history and capacity. Our work is to speak our truth and state our need. Their work is to listen. Whether they can or not is information for us, but it does not change the validity of our experience or the legitimacy of our need. We anchor ourselves in the knowing that our reality is not contingent on their perception of it.

Patience is not passive. It's the active practice of allowing something to unfold at its own pace.

Perhaps this is the most profound shift—the movement from a desperate need for external validation to a quiet, internal practice of patience with our own unfolding. The work was never about crafting the perfect explanation. It was always about learning to listen to the wisdom of our own system, to honor its signals, and to offer ourselves the compassion we crave from the world. The bridge we are building is not, ultimately, to another person, but from the fragmented parts of ourselves back to a place of inner wholeness. It is a slow, tender, and deeply personal return, one that happens not in a single conversation, but over a lifetime of moments, each one an opportunity to meet ourselves with a little more understanding.

Frequently Asked Questions

What's the difference between being triggered and just being upset?

While both involve emotional distress, the distinction lies in proportionality and physiology. Being upset is typically a present-moment reaction to a present-moment event. A trigger, however, is a past event breaking into the present, causing a physiological reaction—like a racing heart or numbness—that is often vastly disproportionate to the current context. It's the body re-experiencing a past threat, not just the mind feeling an emotion. Upset is a feeling; a trigger is a full-body event.

How can I support someone without fully understanding what a trigger feels like?

Support does not require a shared experience, but a willingness to listen and respect their reality. The most powerful thing you can do is ask, "What does support look like for you in those moments?" and then honor their answer. It often involves not taking their response personally, giving them space without pressure, and trusting that they are the expert on their own internal world. Your role is not to fix it, but to offer a calm and steady presence that respects their stated needs and avoids trying to problem-solve their feelings.
